sentences.text: In the last part of 1944, there was an organized group which formed a kangaroo court, so to say, punishing corroborators in a camp and those that came in new transported that were corroborators with the Germans against their fellow Jews.
In certain locations, as I recall, when the Danish Jews were brought in from-- not Danish.
Belgian Jews, I'm sorry.
Belgian Jews.
They pointed out among them a Gestapo informer.
He was taken out by the prisoners and killed.
But before he was killed, he was tortured.
We had good training and things to do, by experts.
He begged to be killed, in fact, after a couple of, a couple days.
Yes.
Yes, I saw it myself.
I didn't take part in it, but I saw it myself.
They also-- we had a sadistic kapos and black leaders before.
Those were also warned by the, by the kangaroo courts, and they stopped.
They got scared and stopped.
Yes.
A fellow that I knew well, a Greek prisoner from Thessaloniki, a young man by the name of Kovo.
That's all I know.
K-O-V-O.
He was a law student in Greece.
He spoke several languages fluently, Spanish, Italian, Greek, of course, Arabic, Hebrew.
He learned German.
He even spoke Polish.
He was a brilliant boy.
He was working in a railroad tracks, a repair group, a repair commando, where a train with the Italian soldiers going to the Eastern Front stopped because of the repairs.
He spoke live in Italian, telling them that he was Italian.
They smuggled him on a plane, put him in a military uniform, and he escaped.
Of course, we were punished.
We had to stay on the appell plots for hours in rain while they were searching, because they didn't know what happened, how he escaped.
But I found out about it later, if I may tell you.
After the war, after I was liberated, I was in Hamburg sitting in a restaurant when a British soldier came by and looked at me and said, hey, Schwarze.
They used to call me Schwarze because I was dark.
I looked it up.
I didn't know who the hell it was.
I say, says, who are you?
He says, Adam, don't you remember me?
I'm Kovo.
What happened was he escaped from Auschwitz, he went on a train to Krakow, which is only, what, 20 miles from Auschwitz.
Jump off the train, joined the Polish underground, had a-- the gold cross tattooed over the number.
Was sent by Polish underground to Warsaw, was fought in Warsaw uprising, Polish uprising.
Was captured by the Germans, escaped from them, joined the British army as a translator, and was in Hamburg as a British uniform soldier.
[LAUGHTER] Great, great ladies man.
Fantastic guy.
I have his photo right here.
Of course, while we worked in the, in the factories, in the engine factories, we were in contact with civilians, with Polish workers, free workers who worked in the power station.
And those that we smuggled goods for and got sometimes bread for, that smuggled back breads.
And they were telling us what they heard.
You know, that the Russian-- the Stalingrad story.
We knew that the German army is retreating, you know?
That the British are bombing Germany already.
And all those things we heard something.
We also heard in-- on the attempt on Hitler's life, because the German radio announced that to the-- not to us, the population.
They told us that there was a, a attempt in the [INAUDIBLE] place, and he survived.
Yes.
We saw American planes flying over by the hundreds, very little in the sky.
In fact, the there was one time-- only one time-- that they bombed Buna because of the military value of it.
Never, ever, were the Allies bombing on the railroad tracks leading to Auschwitz, on the gas chamber of Auschwitz, on the crematoria.
They were well aware.
They knew exactly where things are located.
Of course, Roosevelt's excuse was that the war should be all effort of conquering the Germans and shortening the war.

But one or two raids would have made it extremely difficult for the Germans to bring the, the, the prisoners into the gas chambers, if the railroad tracks were bombed, or the gas chamber, or the gas chamber were bombed or things.
No.
No, because I knew, it's impossible.
One, is that very few, if any, were able to escape.
And those that escaped were Polish people.
There was a, an antagonism, anti-Semitism between if I escape, I may have been you know, announced by a Polish population that I tried to escape too.
Mhm.
What we heard, that it's almost impossible.
And after they captured you, they tortured you, and then you got hung in public.
We had hanging, people trying to escape, or those they thought to escape.
There were [? hunkered ?] in [INAUDIBLE].
At any point while you were in Buna, did you consider a suicide?
No.
No.
I wanted to survive the war.
I was dreaming of surviving the war one day, you know?
I had-- I had good relation, very kind relation, with the master in charge of my work as a wirer of high-tension control cells.
While-- I got sick once.
I had high fever, and he-- I had to go to work, because if you didn't go to work, you went to the, the, the, sick, sick room in Buna, you are on your way to gas chamber.
So you went to work with high fever.
And the master, Mr.
Miller, he noticed I'm sick.
So he hid me in a high-voltage cell, which had a marked on the door, high voltage.
Do not enter.
And had that death-- skeleton with cross arms on it.
And says, lay on the floor, because the SS would never get near.
They were scared.
They see that.
You know, it's danger.
High voltage.
Mhm.
He was kind.
I mean, I couldn't talk to him.
His subordinate, who was a foreman, was a Volksdeutsch.
Volksdeutsch is a German who was a Polish German born in Poland but of German parents [INAUDIBLE].
He was a Nazi.
And we talked to him, and you could-- when they were burning people in Birkenau, you could-- the wind blew our way because we are only a few miles away, you could-- the stink was horrible.
And we tell him, says, you know what they're doing?
You're burning people.
And his answer was, [NON-ENGLISH].
We're going to win.
That was his answer.
And when you win the war, everything--
We left Auschwitz-- the Russians were advancing on Krakow, and they evacuated Buna in January, 1945.
January?
Yeah.
January, 1945.
We are put on a march, on a forced march, to a town called Glievitz.
G-L-I-E-V-I-T-Z.
A former Polish town, Glievitz.
And there-- on a march, whoever couldn't make it during march was shot on the road.
We were put in an open, open freight cars, packed solid.
Not enough space actually to stay on both feet.
That's how tight we are forced to go in there.
And started a two0weeks journey.
No food, no water.
First it was winter.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Those that were near the edge, which my friend that I was together, not friend-- we-- you tried to stick with somebody-- had a metal cup and a string.
As the car moved, he could pick up some snow.
I mean, a spoonful.
Those inside that didn't have water or bread got crazy.
You don't die from hunger that fast, but you go crazy without water.
You go crazy.
You can't stand without water.
With few days, and you're out.
And they would die [INAUDIBLE].
And they would freeze.
And we used to stack them up.
In fact, sit on them.
We went to Poland, part because going forward Austria, we begged Polish railroad workers, and we Poles, we spoke Polish.
Please throw us snow.
And they would turn their back on us.
When we got to a-- near a town, and it's almost like an order, we should throw all the deaths out in front of the towns.
Just heave them out over the thing.
One day, the commandment of the transport, Mengele, you've heard of Dr.
Mengele?
He stopped the train with an automatic in his hand.
He says, I'll kill you all here if you do that one more time.
You're going to throw the dead when I tell you.
And from then on, he used to stop the train in open field and then tell us to dump it over-- over the-- So he was on the transport that you--
He was on the transport.
On our transport going out.
The-- we got-- they, they-- the train went through Poland to Czechoslovakia.
When it entered Czechoslovakia, the Czechs on the ground-- and that's my thought-- must have notified the public.
Every trestle, every bridge, the Czechs, either on foot or on a bicycle were riding and throwing bread and snow into the open cars.
They wonderful people, the Czech.
I found that in Auschwitz, in camp.
They always shared their food, if they had packages of food.
Not the Poles.
They were shooting at them.
The SS were shooting at them.
And they just went on a bicycle, threw it, and run.
We-- they've brought us to a camp, Mauthausen, in Austria.
It's not far from Vienna.
It's one of the worst camps.
It was a, a stone quarry, which people didn't last more than a month.
Used to-- had to carry stones up the hill from the quarry, and they were torturous.
There was one SS Man there-- what I heard, I didn't see it.
Mhm.
He used to save bullets.
He used to take two people, back-to-back, and tell them to open their mouth and stick the pistol in one guy's mouth and blow two people at one time, save a bullet.
And what happened when you got to Mauthausen?
We got there.
They didn't unload us.
Later, we found that Mauthausen was already overcrowded.
They couldn't take any more prisoners.
So we stayed overnight there and started again in the morning, toward Berlin.
And we got to the concentration camp, Sachsenhausen.
As I say, that journey was about two weeks, a little more.
More than 80% of our train were dead, or about 80%.
Very few people, about 20, 22, 23 people came out alive from our wagon, and there were other-- almost 100.
In Sachsenhausen, it was-- I don't know the history-- if you know history.
It was the first German concentration camp.
It's out-- it's on the outskirt, a suburb of Berlin.
Sachsenhausen had a factory attached to it, Heinkel.
Heinkel was an airplane manufacture.
They developed, at least to best of our knowledge, the first jet plane, fighter jet plane.
Heinkel?
H-E-I-N-K-E-L.
Yes.
I was assigned to a-- first, to riveting, where you lay inside the, the tube, that was part of the fuselage of the plane.
And the outside would put a rivet, and you had pneumatic hammers that pressed the rivet.
You could die from the noises like, world come to an end because reverberating in the fuselage.
Later, I was transferred to a German mechanic-- very nice guy-- to read and correct drawings.
He was an engineer.
He come from the east part of Germany, where the Russians already took over.
He lost his family.
He was very kind to me.
He let me sleep under his desk.
He used to sneak some potatoes to me, you know?
There was no food.
There was such a horrendous-- we got one slice of bread a day.
I eat bark off the trees.
I eat pine needles.
I got food poisoning.
My body broke out in red spots.
But nonetheless, you know, working there-- oh, not physical work really-- with him, and getting, you know, sometimes he would bring a couple potatoes, make me survive.
No.
Not at all.
As I say, I have problems.
I remember Miller because it's such a common name, from the other--
We were-- they bombed one time.
They bombed, Allied bombed, the Heinkel works with prisoner, and several persons got killed.
But that was the only thing.
It was really-- some moments were very exciting for us.

Every time there was a British Air Force bomb Berlin at night, and-- [CLEARS THROAT] --they would come first in, I think, fighter planes or spotter planes and drop what we call Christmas trees spotting phosphor of different colors.
And look like a, like a Christmas.
Giant, colorful thing hanging in the air.
And that was markers for the bombers.
Then came the bombers and bomb, and the earth shook.
But we had to run out, out of the barracks, and lay on the ground.
And it was fantastic, you know?
Watching Berlin being bombed, when we are right next to it, and the earth shaking.
Mhm.
The Americans-- [CLEARS THROAT] --bombed that in the daytime, but you didn't see the flares and all these things.
We stayed until April.
The Russian were advanced on, on Berlin, and we were evacuated.
We evacuated-- we are, we are evacuated by foot with no food.
Nothing.
A march.
And again, if you didn't make it, you're dead.
We're marching along the road on the side.
And tens of thousands of prisoners from different concentration camps were marching on the road.
The German army was marching in the middle of the road in either direction.
This arranged the soldiers unshaved, not the glorious, victorious supermen, but beaten, running in all directions.
And the American P-51's used to fly over and machine gun them.
But if they saw a prison colony like ours, they used to fly right over us and gun in front.
And many times they would kill horses.
And by the time our column went by a dead horse, there was nothing left but a skeleton.
We used to cut the meat away.
We took all the German gas mask that the soldiers dropped because they were too heavy, made holes in them.
Put the meat, put wood, and cooked while we walked.
Just moved the mask and, you know, smoked the stuff.
No.
The guards, the guards-- in fact, some nights, we slept in barns of a farm.
They put us in the ring the farm out.
The guards were with us because that's prevented them from being shipped to the front.
As long as they had us, they didn't have to go to front, you know?
So at that point, they really didn't think-- in fact, one time we are in a barn and I and another fellow, we woke up in the morning.
There were no more SS.
They all-- everybody left.
We overslept somewhere.
And we got out and there were German soldiers cooking something.
And we got to them, and they spoke Russian.
They were the Vlasov army.
The, the, the Soviet army with General Vlasov, who deserted and joined the German army.
He was later killed and they were also.
And so I could converse with them, and I spoke Russian.
And they had potatoes, we had salt.
They had no salt.
I don't remember, stole somewhere.
[LAUGHTER] Some other barracks or some other farm that we pass by.
We just have a pocket, packet, handful of salt.
So-- [COUGHS] --in fact-- and we-- we started walking.
An SS man saw us and joined us because that saved his life.
If he walk by himself, that's either a deserter or something.
He got lost also.
It was such a mad.
Anyway, we, we were taken to the group again, and we marched.
When-- on May 1, 1945, about noon time, there was a motorcycle coming on a highway.
We were sitting on the side of the road, resting, whatever.
The German army trucks, trunks, were moving either direction, disarray.
A motorcycle and a German screaming, the Americans are three miles away.
And within minutes, from the other side came another motorcycle without the officers howling the Russians are coming, or are near.
And within seconds, the SS disappeared.
And the Germans on the highway threw off their weapons, threw off their insignia, and run into the woods.
And we are free.
About 15 minutes later, there was an American jeep with an attachment and a machine gun on top, drove slowly by.
Stopped by us.
He had K ration in the attachment, and gave us k rations.
And you know, we started eating.
And we jumped on the German trucks and we found food, and I found a, a German pistol, Luger, and I strapped myself.
I don't know why, 'cause I never used it.
No.
The, the death march from Sachsenhausen down was-- [CLEARS THROAT] --part of it was horrible.
We had no, no food.
We were skeletons, marching, covered up with blankets.
You know, just over your head.
We went through German villages and small towns, because that's how the highway ran.
And the German civilians, they stood there.
They looked at us like some animals or something.
Nobody ever gave us a kind word, or kind look.
Nothing.
No food.
Nothing.
We just, I mean, we drag our feet through it.
Of course, I was very thin.
I was somewhere around 90 pounds.
My mental, my mental state was very good.
I've never lost hope.
I was not in a depression.
I was-- no fear of death at all.
That was a, a sister of mine.
I've seen it so many times.
I've been so close to it.
And I was weak.
In fact, when we were liberated, the American put us into German military barracks and gave us Red Cross packages which had everything in it, chocolate, powder milk, sardines.
And we ate everything.
And people died.
The stomach burst.
I had a horrendous diarrhea.
Everybody had it.
And we learn in concentration camp to stop a diarrhea because you had diarrhea constantly.
You ate such junk.
You ate stuff off the floor.
You ate bark, anything.
You take charcoal.
You burn wood and you eat charcoal, and it stops diarrhea.
That people start dying.
They stopped-- they brought a medical team, the US Army, and they took us off the food and put us on, on broth, and rice, and very mild food.
But I recuperated very fast.
Mhm.
And where did you go after you recuperated?
We were out.
In fact, the first thing we did-- friend of mine and I, we're-- three of us there were.
Two friends and I, all from Warsaw, all went through the same camps.
They live in Germany, at least.
One of them is leaving.
Stefan Szczupak.
I don't want to start spelling it.
Would you want me to spell it?
Yes, if you wouldn't mind.
Stefan, S-T-E-F-A-N.
Mhm.
Szczupak is S-Z-C-Z-U-P-A-K.
That's pike in English.
Szczupak is a fish, pike.
He lives now in Hamburg, Germany.
He married a German girl.
Mhm.
And another Warsaw guy, Sevek, S-E-V-E-K.
I don't know his-- no.
His last name is Dytman.
D-Y-T-M-A-N.
Dytman.
We walked out and somehow got to the railroad station, which was full of luggage, German luggage.
And we ripped them out and we found clothing, because we were in prison clothes.
And we got suits, and we got some food they had down there.
Some salamis hidden among the clothes.
That's Germans that was shipping it from the occupied territories to family.
And we took the whole bunch of stuff.
We got a farm wagon with two horses that was abandoned, just, just standing there.
And we started toward the town of Shwerin.
Shwerin is S-H-W-E-R-I-N.
We got a, a room in Shwerin.
We used to drive the horses to a village nearby, and I had my luger, my German pistol, strapped to the side, with a prison jacket still on.
I wore it as an emblem.
Get to the mayor and do it the German way.
Look at a watch, which we stole.
And says, you got 10 minutes, and we want X amount of X, bread, butter, chicken.
And boy, they were-- looked at us and were jumping.
That was because the Russian prisoners used to go to the village and blow it out with bazookas.
House to house.
Or put the families against the wall and machine gun them.
The hatred was so horrendous.
I couldn't kill.
I just-- I could.
If I knew who was the one that killed my parents or the one that-- things I could-- but I couldn't just kill people.
We stayed in Shwerin for a few weeks.
I doubled my weight.
The photograph that you see of mine, which was about six months later, was almost double the weight.
My hair started growing again, of course.
We moved away from Shwerin because we heard that the Russian will take over that town.
It was the agreement between the American and the Russian.
And they-- this was part of the Russian zone, the Soviet zone.
So we moved out and moved to a town, Germantown Lubec.
That's L-U-B-E-C.
And how long did you stay in Lubec?
And in Lubec, we only stayed very short time, perhaps a week.
When we next moved to Hamburg.
And in Hamburg, was already under British occupation, British control, British zone of occupation.
They already organized the British assembly place, or a thing for survivors, concentration camp survivors.
We were assigned an apartment, two-room apartment in Hamburg.
We were given a-- coupons for civilian clothing, for one suit, one pair of shoes, to German stores they had to issue.
And we were out on our own.
Yes.
After we were in Hamburg for a while-- [CLEARS THROAT] [COUGHS] --we are in contact with the, with the Palestine, the Jewish Brigade, that was fighting for the British army.
With the British army.
They came in.
And I register for aliyah to go to Palestine.
I didn't want to go back to Poland.
There was nothing there.
I knew my family was killed.
It was no-- something that I go there and find somebody.
There was nobody.
Everyone I knew was murdered.
I registered to go to Palestine.
At that time, it was through Cyprus, that the British diverted the, the thing.
But something crazy happened.
I was with the two boys, and we met a Jewish girl.
Her name was Nina-- N-I-N-A, last name a Rodznek, R-O-D-Z-N-E-K-- who was a survivor and she has very bad asthma.
And the doctor-- we took her to a doctor, and the doctor said, she must go to a drier climate, to a better-- to be able to breathe, because Hamburg is very moist-- it's a port.
It's right on the sea.
So friend one of the three of us, Sevek, took her to Bad Nauheim.
It's near Frankfurt.
It's a resort area, German.
In Bad Nauheim, it was during the Hebrew holiday of Lag BaOmer, which is the, the harvest holiday.
And there are, there are picnics outside.
And there was very small, just dozen or so, survivors living.
